summary: Fiery Deal, jam‑filled Shakedown>Samson and a standout Hand Jive
blurb: The Spectrum show delivers scorching versions of Deal and Comes A Time, with the second set launching into a powerful Shakedown Street > Samson sequence that flows into The Other One. Jerry’s solos are on fire while Brent adds eerie high‑note textures. Hand Jive shines as an audience‑recording highlight.
review: On March 23, 1986 at Philadelphia’s Spectrum the Dead served up a solid mid‑80s performance that still feels fresh. The first set kicks off with a punchy Deal and rolls through tight numbers like Gimme Some Lovin' and Willie And The Hand Jive, but it’s Jerry Garcia’s fiery soloing on Comes A Time that really lights the night. The second set erupts with Shakedown Street into Samson & Delilah, building momentum into an expansive Spoonful jam before the drums give way to a soaring The Other One. Brent Mydland’s eerie sustained high notes add an otherworldly sheen, while Mickey and Bill keep the rhythmic engine humming. Highlights round out with a smoking Good Lovin' and a memorable Hand Jive captured brilliantly on one of the best audience tapes of the ’86 tour. For Deadheads looking for a quintessential mid‑80s set with strong vocals, tight jams, and that signature Spectrum energy, this show is a must‑listen.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Consistent positive sentiment across nine recordings, with multiple reviewers praising standout performances (Deal, Comes A Time, Shakedown>Samson, Hand Jive) and noting only minor mixed comments. No significant negative criticism was recorded, warranting a strong positive adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Good audience source with solid clarity, captures the full show and highlights like Comes A Time, Deal, Hand Jive and Shakedown > Samson; widely praised as the most complete and listenable version.

key_highlights: Scorching Deal and intense Comes A Time open the set
Shakedown Street > Samson launch fuels a jam‑filled second set with Spoonful and The Other One
Hand Jive stands out on one of the best audience recordings of the ’86 tour
Brent’s eerie sustained high notes add an otherworldly texture throughout

band_performance.Jerry: Fiery soloing and perfect tone dominate the night, with standout leads on Deal, Comes A Time and a playful Truckin' tease in Spoonful; occasional lyric flubs don’t detract from his energy.
band_performance.Phil: Provides solid bass foundation and adds vocal depth, especially noticeable during The Other One.
band_performance.Bob: Adds quirky spoken intro before Day Job, lending character to the set.
band_performance.Brent: Creates eerie, sustained high‑note textures that give parts of the show an Enó‑esque, otherworldly feel.
band_performance.Mickey: Delivers strange sustained high‑note sounds that blend with Brent’s keyboards, contributing to the show's unique atmosphere while keeping solid rhythmic support.
